planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost for a spring crop, or direct sow in late summer for fall harvest; De Cicco is an Italian heirloom broccoli from the Lazio region dating to at least 1890, producing a small central head followed by an abundant, continuous harvest of tender sideshoots that can continue for weeks — the classic sprouting broccoli of Italian tradition.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant when daytime temperatures are below 75°F and after hardening off. De Cicco is one of America's oldest and most beloved heirloom broccolis, introduced to the US from the Lazio region of Italy and appearing in seed catalogs since at least 1890; unlike modern large-headed hybrids, De Cicco produces a smaller, open, bluish-green central head (4–6 inches) that is best harvested before flowers open, then rebounds with a prolific and sustained harvest of tender, slender sideshoots that are more delicate and sweet-flavored than the central head — this sideshot harvest can continue for weeks in cool weather, making De Cicco arguably more productive than single-heading types over the full season. The tender sideshoots (broccoletti) are the classic vegetable of Italian cooking braised with olive oil and garlic. An outstanding variety for Gulf Coast fall and winter production and for cool-climate spring gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost for spring planting, or sow in midsummer for a fall crop; Waltham 29 is a classic American open-pollinated broccoli developed by the Massachusetts Agricultural Experiment Station and released in 1954 — long the standard garden broccoli before modern hybrids and still prized for heat tolerance, blue-green color, and reliable production.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after hardening off when temperatures are consistently below 80°F during the day. Waltham 29 broccoli was developed by the Waltham Field Station of the Massachusetts Agricultural Experiment Station and released in 1954 as an improvement over earlier open-pollinated varieties; for decades before hybrid broccoli varieties became dominant in the 1970s and 80s, Waltham 29 was the standard home garden and market broccoli. It produces a large, domed, blue-green to grey-green central head of excellent flavor and tight bead formation, followed by adequate sideshot production; it is notably more tolerant of fall frost than many varieties and holds well in the field without yellowing. Still widely grown for its reliable open-pollinated nature (seed-saveable), cold tolerance, and classic blue-green broccoli flavor. Excellent for fall production in zones 5–11.
sources: University of Massachusetts Extension Vegetable Notes

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or in late summer for a fall crop; rapini is not a true broccoli but a turnip-family vegetable grown for its tender stems, buds, and leaves with a distinctive pleasantly bitter flavor that is essential in Italian cuisine — harvest before the flower buds open for the most tender and flavorful results.

directions: Direct sow thinly in early spring or late summer in rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil in full sun; thin to 6 inches. Rapini (broccoli rabe, cime di rapa, or in dialect rabé) is a fast-growing cool-season vegetable in the Brassica rapa family, more closely related to turnips than to true broccoli; the tender stems, small leaves, and unopened flower buds are harvested together as a bundle when the buds are still tightly closed (40–60 days from seed). The flavor is distinctive — pleasantly bitter, peppery, and slightly nutty — and is considered an essential element of Italian-American and southern Italian cooking: rapini sautéed with garlic, olive oil, and red pepper flakes is the classic accompaniment to sausage, pasta, and orecchiette in Pugliese and Neapolitan traditions. In warmer climates (zones 8–11) it is an outstanding cool-season crop for fall-through-spring production, bolting quickly in heat.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or in late summer for fall harvest; gai lan is one of the most heat-tolerant and productive of the Asian brassica vegetables, more bolt-resistant than most and a particularly good choice for Gulf Coast fall and spring production in zones 8–11.

directions: Direct sow in early spring or late summer in fertile, moist, well-drained soil; thin to 9–12 inches for best results. Gai lan (also written kai-lan or gai laan) is the Chinese broccoli or Chinese kale of Cantonese cuisine, a leafy brassica producing thick, succulent stems, broad blue-green leaves, and small clusters of white flower buds that are harvested together as the edible vegetable; unlike Italian broccoli where the heads are the prize, gai lan is prized for its juicy, thick stems with a clean, mild, slightly bitter flavor that takes well to the intense heat of a wok and oyster sauce. Harvest when the first buds are just beginning to open for peak flavor and tenderness; the plants regrow from the base for additional cuts. More heat-tolerant than most brassicas and an excellent vegetable for Gulf Coast fall and spring seasons when traditional broccoli bolts quickly. Essential in Chinese restaurant cooking — the vegetable in classic 'Chinese broccoli with oyster sauce.'
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension Asian Vegetables
Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring, or in late summer for fall harvest; Bull's Blood is a Victorian-era heirloom beet grown as much for its strikingly deep burgundy-red, ornamental foliage as for the edible roots, making it one of the most beautiful of all vegetable garden plants and an outstanding edging or salad green.

directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep directly in the garden 2–4 weeks before last frost in fertile, deeply worked, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil; soak seeds overnight to improve germination and thin to 3–4 inches when plants are 3 inches tall. Bull's Blood is a Victorian-era heirloom beet first listed in Burpee's catalog in the 1840s, grown for 175+ years for its spectacular deep burgundy-red to near-purple foliage that is among the most vivid of any vegetable; the young leaves and stems are intensely colored and are prized as a microgreen, baby salad leaf, and ornamental edging plant. The roots are also deep red, sweet, and excellent at any size from marble (35–40 days) to mature golf ball (55–60 days). The dramatic foliage remains strongly colored through the season and is highly heat-tolerant for a beet. An outstanding dual-purpose ornamental-vegetable for edible landscaping, container growing, and intensive salad gardens.
sources: W. Atlee Burpee Company catalog archives
